News.title: Four in 10 parents consider listening to audiobooks as ‘cheating’, research says
News.content:
BOOK MARKED Four in 10 parents consider listening to audiobooks as ‘cheating’, research says

FOUR in 10 parents consider listening to audiobooks as ‘cheating’ – but an expert has revealed it can be as effective for children as traditional physical books.

Research of 2,000 mums and dads of kids aged three to 12 revealed 35% don’t believe hearing a story has the same impact as reading print books.



But education and parenting expert Cathy Walker thinks otherwise, explaining how listening still provides them with exposure to more words to absorb and, in turn, helps increase their vocabulary among other things.



Interestingly, of the 94% whose children listen to audiobooks, boys were the bigger adopters, gravitating to them more ahead of girls.

Cathy Walker was speaking in collaboration with kid’s audio player company Yoto, the official audio partner for World Book Day, which has a collection of over 60 audio cards where £1 will be donated from sales to the reading charity.

She said: “Listening to audiobooks is in no way cheating.

“When children lose themselves in a story, whether reading or listening, so much is at play – they are not just absorbing words, they are developing empathy, inhabiting other people’s experiences and learning to understand the world from perspectives that are not their own.



“These are the very skills that will matter most as young people step into an increasingly tech-dominated and volatile world where human connection is absolutely essential.”

The study also revealed 39% of parents believe kids absorb more information from reading than they do an audiobook – but three in 10 disagree.

Interestingly, 37% claim their child’s comprehension is better when they listen to a story compared to when reading one.

And 40% reckon audiobooks help with their little one’s pronunciation of words, as well as improving their listening skills (47%).



Something which 44% of those polled via OnePoll.com have noticed to be a big improvement in since their son or daughter started listening to stories out loud.

Other things parents have picked up on since introducing audiobooks include better vocabulary (38%), spelling and grammar (29%), and a keenness to read (23%).

Cathy also explained a child who listens to stories regularly is not taking a shortcut, but rather the opposite.

According to the education and parenting expert they are building profound experiences and skills as well as the ability to listen actively, engage deeply with ideas, empathise with others, and to be happy in their own company.

She explained audiobooks help them gain the social skills and capital that will afford them the quiet confidence to be themselves, whoever they are with and wherever life takes them.



It also emerged that while 46% would prefer their child to read books, they agree listening to audiobooks is a good alternative – particularly to screen-based activities (39%).

The most common times children listen to audiobooks are in the evening before bed (33%), during car journeys (28%), and while on holiday (22%).

With adventure being the most popular genre (26%), followed by fairytales (24%), bedtime stories (24%) – and classic children’s literature (20%).

Cathy added: “Books in any format expose children to a rich and varied vocabulary that builds on the conversations they have every day with family, friends and teachers.

“This ever-growing language and expression become the foundation for how confidently they communicate, connect, and collaborate throughout their lives.



“As parents, we would want to raise children who can listen well, speak confidently, and genuinely connect with the people around them – stories, whether heard or read, are a crucial part of how we get there.”

For one day only, World Book Day, on Thursday 5th March, Yoto is offering a range of audiobooks for just £1 as well as providing a selection of free audio stories hosted on the World Book Day website.

The audio cards can be played on the free parental app as well as on the company’s screen-free players.

Jess Tarrant, content director at Yoto, added: “We want to ensure audiobooks are as accessible as possible for children and parents to experience.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“Listening to stories doesn’t just need to be for the kids, it can be for the family as a whole.



“We believe audio can have a transformative power, and can help children who perhaps aren’t as drawn to physical books engage with some incredible stories and content they might not otherwise get to experience.”

News.title: Iran shows off underground ‘Missile City’ of suicide drones & rockets wreaking havoc across Middle East in chilling vid
News.content:
IRAN has showed off its sprawling “Missile City” packed with suicide drones and deadly rockets as Tehran wreaks havoc across the Middle East.

Chilling propaganda footage shows a seemingly endless network of tunnels holding rows of devastating bombs and terrifying Shahed drones.





It comes amid mounting fears that the Gulf states, who are currently under Iranian fire amid the spiralling Middle East conflict, could run out of anti-drone weaponry.

Follow our live blog here…

US-backed Gulf countries have burned through their defensive stockpiles at breakneck speed amid waves of Iranian missiles and drones.

In the ominous clip, a ticking clock can be heard in the background as drone footage captures the extent of Iran‘s arsenal.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps’ bunker contains a huge picture of late Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed in a US-Israeli strike on Saturday, overlooking the massive array of weapons.

Iranian flags can be seen hanging from the ceiling as lorries are also seen carrying Shahed drone launchers.

Shahed drones, which cost just tens of thousands to produce and take little time to manufacture, are also used by Vladimir Putin for his war in Ukraine.

On Wednesday, Israel made a bleak admission that Tehran still has a significant capacity to fire missiles at its enemies.



It also comes amid mounting fears that anti-drone tech is simply too expensive to last an extended military campaign.

It comes as…

For example, the American-made Patriot missiles can cost up to £3.75million to produce – and that’s without export costs.

On the other hand, a single deadly drone can be as cheap as £16,000 – meaning the Iranian regime’s attack campaign will be much easier to sustain.

THAAD missile batteries, another defensive system, can even be priced at up to £10million.



Amid Iran’s ongoing drone onslaught targeting US bases across the Gulf states, millions of dollars have been spent intercepting the strikes.

The United Arab Emirates at the time of investigating had shot down 92 per cent of the 541 projectiles fired its way – and this had reportedly cost them up to £567million.

Interceptors can cost up to £3million to take down just one drone, reports say.



The Iranian regime on the other hand had spent as little as £8million on this fleet of drones targeted at the UAE.

Tehran is reportedly firing more than 2,500 drones a day, flooding air defences in a strategy designed to overwhelm them.

While most incoming projectiles have been shot down, a handful have slipped through – including a drone strike that rocked the US Consulate in Dubai.

A source told the Daily Mail: “At the current rates the supplies could run out within four days… The interceptors are being used at an unprecedented speed.”

Since the campaign began, the UAE says it has been targeted by around 200 ballistic missiles, eight cruise missiles and 689 drones.



While all missiles were intercepted, 44 drones still managed to strike the country, with Bahrain, Kuwait and Qatar also facing heavy barrages.

As explosions ripple across the region, desperate Brits are racing to reach the first UK evacuation flight leaving Oman tonight.

A Government-chartered plane will depart Muscat International Airport at 11pm local time (7pm GMT), prioritising the most vulnerable passengers.



British nationals, their partners and children under 18 with valid travel documents are eligible.

But the Foreign Office warned people not to turn up unless contacted directly.

The scramble comes after around 130,000 Brits registered as stranded in the Gulf as airspace closures cripple travel.

More than 11,000 commercial flights have been cancelled, disrupting journeys for roughly one million people.

Panicked expats have been driving across the desert from Dubai to neighbouring Oman or Saudi Arabia in a bid to escape.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper told MPs the “safety and security” of British nationals was the Government’s “top priority”.

Airlines are now rushing to restart limited routes out of the region.

News.title: Married female PE teacher, 32, admits having sex with teen student and sexually assaulting another boy, 16
News.content:
SICK MISS Married female PE teacher, 32, admits having sex with teen student and sexually assaulting another boy, 16

A NEWLYWED PE teacher has admitted she had sex with her teen student and sexually abused another.

The shock admission by Australian educator Tayla Brailey, 32, came as she pleaded guilty to sex abuse charges in court on Wednesday.





Brailey had sex with a male student, 16, in a New South Wales suburb in June 2024, the court heard.

A month later, she sexually touched another male student, 17.

At the time, Brailey was a newlywed, having walked down the aisle less than 12 months earlier in a lavish ceremony.



The former high school teacher was first arrested in August 2024 and was released on bail on strict conditions she move out of the home she shared with her husband and in with her parents.

Less than two weeks later she was re-arrested on additional charges for abusing a second victim.

Entering a guilty plea at the Campbelltown Local Court, Brailey also admitted to two counts of using a carriage service to solicit or transmit child abuse material.

Brailey’s defense lawyer, Abbas Soukie, told the court the agreed facts of the case were still “disputed”.



In previous court documents reported by 7News, it was revealed Brailey had sent sexually explicit images and videos of herself to her 16-year-old student via Snapchat.

She then invited the teen over to her house.

It was there that police said she first sexually abused him by performing a sex act on him.

His friend who had accompanied the victim saw the abuse taking place, The Daily Mail reported.

Investigators believe the pair then met up a second time and had unprotected sex in the back of a car.



According to the documents, Brailey then allegedly drove to a chemist and bought the morning-after pill.

The abuse of the second student took place in a classroom, police said.

Later, investigators said Brailey had attempted to incite him to have sex with her.

At the time of her arrest, the NSW Department of Education said Brailey was suspended from work without pay and support was being provided to students and staff at the school.

Brailey will be sentenced on March 26.

News.title: ‘Knifeman’ stabbed man in ‘siege’ outside nursery before barricading himself in flats as bloodied woman screamed inside
News.content:
KNIFE 'SIEGE' ‘Knifeman’ stabbed man in ‘siege’ outside nursery before barricading himself in flats as bloodied woman screamed inside

AN alleged knifeman stabbed a man outside a nursery before reportedly barricading himself in flats as a woman screamed from inside.

Cops rushed to a street yesterday after reports of a man being attacked in the street as a nearby school plunged into lockdown.





He then ran into a block of flats near to Rosary Catholic Primary School in Birmingham and barricaded himself inside.

A woman with blood on her hands was seen screaming from a window that a man was outside her door, The Telegraph reports.

Armed officers swooped on the area outside the school and reportedly told locals it was a “siege”, before arresting a 32-year-old man.



Cops confirmed that a man in his 20s has tragically died and a murder investigation has been launched.

A woman suffered injuries that were not life-threatening.

West Midlands Police confirmed firearms cops were deployed as part of the urgent response.

Detective Superintendent James Munro from West Midlands Police, said: “Our thoughts remain with the man’s family at this time.



“We believe this to be a contained incident with a man in custody.

“We are reviewing CCTV footage and speaking to a number of people in the area. But would appeal to anyone who has any information to get in touch.

“We understand how deeply distressing and concerning this incident is and we will have extra officers in the area to offer reassurance to the community.”

West Midlands Police have called on anyone with more information to come forward.

Witnesses or people with mobile phone or dashcam footage have been urged to contact the force on 101 quoting log number 2105 of March 3.



Alternatively, calls can be made to Crimestoppers anonymously on 0800 555111.

News.title: Fears vengeful Iran has activated sleeper agents INSIDE Britain – while Tehran’s hacker army ‘is ready to attack UK’
News.content:
BRITS have been warned that Iran has triggered sleeper agents inside the UK as Tehran-backed cyber warriors look to unleash chaos.

Security experts have revealed a murky web of military units and “sleeper cell” sympathisers are ready to attack Britain as regional tensions reach breaking point.





The National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) this week warned British firms to strengthen their cyber security following US-Israeli strikes on Iran and the death of supreme leader Ali Khamenei.

The NCSC said organisations should “prepare to respond” to “Iran-linked hacktivists”.

And in a disturbing development, top security analysts warn the regime’s influence may already be active within our borders.



Dr. Andreas Krieg, a leading security professor at King’s College London, warned it’s “plausible” that individuals with ties to the Basij – Iran’s notorious paramilitary militia – could be operating on UK soil.

While they may not be in uniform, Dr. Krieg warns these “low-visibility” agents are experts in stalking dissidents and activists, moving money and causing chaos online.

Dr Krieg said: “Periods of heightened confrontation create incentives for Tehran to demonstrate reach.

“A leadership shake-up can trigger riskier signalling if decision-makers feel threatened.”



He said that the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) use overseas individuals with “Basij ties, sympathies, or past service who can be leveraged for low-visibility tasks”.

Dr Krieg said: “In the UK context, the more credible risk is that such individuals contribute to surveillance of dissidents, intimidation and coercion.”

And escalation increases the risk of these operations as competition between competing powers intensifies and command control weakens.

This leads to “actors interpreting vague guidance aggressively”, Dr Krieg added.



Dr Krieg believes that the current situation in Iran is likely to lead to an “intensification” of monitoring, harassment, and disruption of opposition figures, along with “contingency planning for retaliatory options”.

Meanwhile experts who have been tracking Iranian cyber criminals for 15 years warn their tactics are becoming increasingly “aggressive.”

Unlike the precision of Russian or Chinese hackers, the Iranian cyber “playbook” relies on online scamming to spread viruses.



Rafe Pilling, director of threat intelligence at IT security firm Sophos X-Ops said: “We’ve been tracking and blocking Iranian‑backed cyber-attacks for nearly 15 years.

“Their most successful attacks rely on tricking people into infecting themselves through emails, chat apps, social media, or video calls.

“Recent military action has also disrupted Iran’s intelligence leadership, creating disorganisation in the short‑term that makes coordinated cyber operations harder.”

It comes as…



He added: “Iran doesn’t have the scale or resources Russia or China do…their operations are generally noisier and more aggressive.

“They also lean heavily on social engineering, impersonating friends or colleagues to build trust before sending malicious phishing links or computer viruses.”

Pilling said that he had observed a “spectrum” of Iran-backed cyber attacks over the past few years.

One of the most common is “multi-stage phishing campaigns” where attackers craft documents that appear highly relevant to the recipient’s interests, embedding malware or linking to harmful websites they have created.



He added: “In addition, we’ve seen the use of legitimate Remote Management and Monitoring (RMM) tools. By deploying these tools as payloads, attackers can discreetly access and monitor organisational systems, maintaining an ongoing presence without attracting immediate attention.

“This approach allows them to establish a persistent foothold, sometimes for extended periods, while remaining under the radar.”

But he feels the most “concerning” is Iranian hackers’ focus on messaging accounts such as Telegram.

Pilling said: “Once attackers compromise an account, they methodically map the victim’s contacts and use this information to widen their influence, targeting friends, relatives, and colleagues.

“A related tactic involves malware engineered specifically to steal Telegram credentials. With control of the account, attackers can further exploit it for malicious purposes, amplifying the impact across the victim’s network.”



Steps to protect YOUR cyber security in wake of Iran threat Rafe Pilling, director of threat intelligence at IT security firm Sophos X-Ops, advises individuals and businesses to: Be suspicious of pressure. If a message pushes you to make a quick financial decision, stop, it may be a scam. Use built‑in password safes and passkeys on your phone to secure email, banking, and other sensitive accounts. Keep antivirus and software up to date across phones, apps, tablets, and computers.

Another cyber attack by the Cyber Av3ngers, an anti-Israeli hacktivist group, run by the Iran’s IRGC, impacted a variety of industrial systems around the world including water companies in the US and Romania.

While the NCSC warns that Iranian hackers have previously targeted “Industrial Control Systems” – the digital brains behind our water, gas, and electricity supplies – Pilling believe the UK’s “extra safety layers” are currently holding firm.

However, the risk remains a “fast-moving” threat. If a British energy giant’s supply chain in the Middle East is decapitated by a cyber-strike, the ripple effect could hit UK wallets, compounding the risk already posed by military blockades in the Strait of Hormuz.

Phil Cotter, CEO of SmartSearch – an anti-money laundering firm – says the IRGC’s model is “designed” for this kind of cyber warfare and may use the same organised crime networks it has used for years to evade sanctions, move weapons and smuggle drugs and oil.

He said: “They’ve spent years developing sophisticated evasion infrastructure using criminal proxies for plausible deniability.



“Historically, when tensions escalate, sanctioned regimes increasingly rely on proxy networks, turning to criminals they’ve cultivated and essentially saying: ‘We’ll pay you to do this for us.’

“This extends to cyber operations. Iran maintains state-sponsored cyber capability linked to the IRGC and Ministry of Intelligence.

“Well-known groups include APT33, APT34, APT35 (Charming Kitten), APT39, and MuddyWater, attributed by the US, EU, UK, and Israel to Iranian state actors conducting espionage, sabotage, and surveillance.”



What is the UK terror level? IRAN poses a real threat to normal Brits with terrorists plotting “death to England” on our streets, a leading Iran expert has warned. Sir Keir Starmer has been told he must be honest after joining Trump’s blitz on the Ayatollah’s twisted regime. The UK has allowed the US military to use British airbases for “specific and limited defence” – with a major RAF base in Cyprus struck by an Iranian drone yesterday. Even so, the PM has fallen short of raising Britain’s terror threat level from “substantial” to “severe” or “critical”. Now, Starmer has been told he must be honest with the Britain about the very real threat on UK’s streets. Kasra Aarabi – a specialist in Iranian military and security affairs – told The Sun: “Keir Starmer has a responsibility as Prime Minister to explain the nature of the threat, the threat that this regime poses to people in Britain. “It’s not a threat confined to Iran. It’s not a threat confined to the Middle East. It is a direct threat to British national security, to Britain’s interests. “This is a regime that has the slogan ‘death to England‘.”

He believes Iran will use money laundering through their existing criminal networks to fund cyber warfare.

Cotter added: “Even cyber operations need funding – operatives, infrastructure, tools.

“Rather than the IRGC directly funding through traceable channels, they use criminal intermediaries who’ve been moving money for them for years, the same networks handling drug trafficking from Afghanistan to Europe or oil smuggling can move funds for cyber operations.

“The IRGC’s dominance over Iranian economic sectors enables them to coerce firms into acting as cut-outs.

“So, UK threats likely won’t be direct, obvious Iranian attacks, they’ll come through criminal proxies who’ve been working with IRGC-linked entities for years on sanctions evasion, now potentially asked to conduct cyber operations for payment.



“It looks like organised cybercrime rather than state-sponsored activity, even though funding and tasking ultimately come from IRGC elements or Iranian intelligence. This creates plausible deniability and makes attribution harder.”

He says the answer is to “disrupt” the flow of money by screening financial transactions from fraudulent companies – some of which may look British-owned but are actually linked to the IRGC.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Cotter said: “Disrupting money flows through identity verification and sanctions screening makes it harder to fund operations. Whether this is cyber-attacks, terrorism, or weapons procurement.

“Financial institutions need heightened vigilance – not just looking for obviously Iranian names, but understanding these complex proxy relationships and IRGC-linked criminal networks.”

News.title: Fire chief sacked after throwing disabled YouTuber’s phone over hedge at fire he was filming wins £4k payout
News.content:
A FIRE chief who was fired after he threw a disabled YouTuber’s phone over a hedge has been awarded a £4,000 payout.

John Linden, 53, got into an “altercation” with blogger Jimmy Evans when he tried to move the Youtuber away from the scene of a fire.





Mr Evans – a self-styled journalist – was filming the blaze for his social media channel and became “aggressive and obstructive” towards the veteran firefighter.

Mr Linden was prompted to “grab” the blogger’s phone and hurl it over a nearby hedge in June 2023.

The video was uploaded by Mr Evans, who is disabled, to his channel Wheelz Media and he reported the fire chief.



After 32 years of service, Mr Linden was sacked for gross misconduct.

The former firefighter won an unfair dismissal case against Norfolk Fire and Rescue Service, was awarded £3,938 and may receive more.

Mr Linden was made station manager of the King’s Lynn fire station in 2023, the tribunal heard.

The department was aware of Mr Evans and Mr Linden knew him to be “challenging and abusive”.



Employment Judge Andrew Spencer said: “In recent years, [Mr Linden] and his work colleagues have had to deal with self-proclaimed social media ‘auditors’.”

He explained how the “auditors” show up in public spaces and record emergency workers before uploading the footage to social media.

Some of these bloggers will act aggressively and obstructively in order to get a rise out of the subject.

Such videos can then be monetised through social media channels, the judge added.

The tribunal saw the video Mr Evans took where the fire chief can be heard “politely and calmly” asking the blogger to move back to where it was safe.



Mr Evans, who was in a mobility scooter, refused to move and said it was “publicly accessible” and he was “legally allowed” to be there.

When Mr Linden proposed calling the police, Mr Evans pushed his phone into the firefighter’s face.



Mr Linden then “grabbed” the phone and can be seen walking down the road before tossing it into a hedge.

The YouTuber complained to the Norfolk fire department and said he could not find his phone when he went back to the scene with his girlfriend.

Mr Evans was compensated £300 for the loss of his phone and told the service would undertake an internal investigation.

After the investigation conducted by Peter Rowe, Mr Linden was fired in August 2023.

The judge found that Mr Linden was unfairly dismissed due to procedural failures during the investigation and said it should have been more “rigorous”.



He noted the police fire chief’s “inappropriate” request that Mr Rowe shouldn’t interview members of the crew who witnessed the “altercation”.

Judge Simpson also criticised how Mr Evans was not asked for a full video of the incident.

Following the tribunal, Mr Linden – who had won several commendations – said he had lost “more than a job” when he was sacked.

He added: “I had worked very hard throughout my career. I ended up having PTSD and I have faced a lot of aggressive people.

“That particular person had caused issues to us before and had caused issues to the police before.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

“There was the aggression, just how dynamic it was. It was in a split second that he got aggressive with me and I lost a 32 year career.”

Mr Linden was awarded £3,938 and a further compensation fee is yet to be determined.

News.title: Expect a long war - new Iran leader Motjaba Khamenei is a tough hardliner
News.content:
Expect a long war - new Iran leader Motjaba Khamenei is a tough hardliner

The likely appointment of Iran's new leader, the super-wealthy second son of the slain Ayatollah, solidifies the power of Tehran's shadowy military elite - the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.

View 5 Images Supporters of the hardline regime applaud Motjaba Khamenei (Image: ABEDIN TAHERKENAREH/EPA/Shutterstock)

Iran's probable new multi-billionaire hardliner Supreme Leader already has a huge and ominous Israeli target on his head and his appointment is a clear sign of Tehran’s defiant war posture. Sources suggest war veteran Motjaba Khamenei is very unlikely to bend to Israel and the US’s force and he will want to spin out this conflict, exporting as much violence as possible around the region.

‌



He survived five days of military strikes by moving around in Tehran’s vast tunnel network, thought to be hundreds of metres deep, so far down it can take minutes to reach by elevator. It is believed his thoroughly modern appointment, thought to be a foregone conclusion, was made unusually after just two remote and virtual meetings held by Tehran’s Assembly of experts. He has led Tehran's security apparatus for years.

‌



View 5 Images In the shadows: Likely leader Motjaba Khamenei (Image: NurPhoto via Getty Images)

‌



‌



The remote meetings were set up after the intended site for the vote in a place called Qum was bombed by the US and Israel. Although fantastically wealthy through his property empire Motjaba, second son of the slain late Ayatollah, is the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps leader of choice.

His popularity with the hardline zealots is ironic given his lifestyle and the immense wealth he has selected for himself as a member of Tehran’s elite. Heavily sanctioned by the US the 56 year-old was born into a life of wealth and comfort but has taken on much of his hate-cleric father’s hard view on the west.

View 5 Images Airstrike on Ferdowsi Square, Central Tehran (Image: Parspix/ABACA/Shutterstock)

‌



But despite his tough stance on the west, further hardened by the death of his wife Zahra Haddad-Adel in an Israel-US air-strike, he does have a more worldly view. The father-of-three has a reported £110million property somewhere in the UK and his wealth has been pumped into multiple Swiss bank accounts in recent years. His mother Mansoureh Khojasteh Bagherzadeh, and one of his sons were killed alongside his father in strikes on Saturday.

As the second son of Khamenei he has long been seen as a potential successor and it is believed he is an arch strategist also schooled in his father’s beliefs in theocracy. For years he has exerted behind-the-scenes power in the running of Tehran and developed very close ties with the shadowy IRGC, Iran’s elite military force.

View 5 Images Smoke rises after strike on Tehran (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

‌



He has for some time been shadowing his father’s running of the country, taking on much of the responsibility whilst the late Ayatollah was sheltering. As a mid-ranking cleric he also served in Iran’s armed forces during the Iran-Iraq war in the 1980’s, during which he forged friendships and loyalty with the IRGC.

Mojtaba was chosen by the country's so-called "Assembly of Experts" under pressure from the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps sources suggest. Despite never holding a formal position within his father's regime, it is widely believed he holds significant influence within the Islamic Republic's political and security spheres.

View 5 Images US Super Hornet lands on USS Abraham Lincoln (Image: Getty Images)

Article continues below

His late wife was the daughter of a leading Tehran politician and society figure. And he is firmly in the sights of Israel already. Israeli politician israel Katz said: “Any leader selected by the Iranian terror regime to continue leading the plan for Israel's destruction, threatening the United States, the free world and countries in the region, and suppressing the Iranian people, will be a certain target for assassination, no matter his name or where he hides.” It is believed he has already been targeted by US-Israeli strikes since the war began with air attacks on Saturday.

News.title: Dunelm's 'beautiful' £20 buy makes front of any house 'look stunning all year'
News.content:
Dunelm's 'beautiful' £20 buy makes front of any house 'look stunning all year'

All customer reviews so far are five stars, as shoppers say "you won't be disappointed"

View 3 Images Shoppers can order the 'stunning' wreath online (Image: Getty )

Dunelm is selling a £20 item that shoppers say is so good they claim "you won't be disappointed". The homeware store is a popular choice across the country for its wide variety of household supplies, including decorations and ornaments.

With the sun coming out more and spring on the way, many people are sprucing up their homes and gardens for the warmer weather. One decoration that is becoming increasingly popular in the UK is the front door wreath, which isn't just for Christmas.

Dunelm is selling an Artificial Outdoor Ivy Wreath for £20 that has been getting five-star reviews from pleased shoppers. The product description reads: " Ideal for both outdoor and indoor use, this stunning 50cm Ivy Wreath boasts a natural, realistic look and is weather resistant. Finished in a natural green colour, this wreath is perfect for hanging in any space."

Numerous customers say the £20 wreath looks "beautiful" on their front door, making their house instantly look better from the outside. Others say it is "great" for use all year round, "not just for Christmas", and "looks more expensive than it is".

Besides Dunelm, several other retailers are selling similar wreaths. The Range is selling a 50cm Green Eucalyptus Wreath for £9.99, which "instantly brightens your space with charm to create a cosy ambience".

View 3 Images Dunelm's £20 Artificial Outdoor Ivy Wreath (Image: Dunelm)

Amazon is also selling a Plaid Bow Green Leaves Wreath for £18.99, which can be purchased without the bow for a pound less. Elsewhere, B&M has a cream Boho Wreath on sale for £12.

However, Dunelm's £20 Artificial Outdoor Ivy Wreath has plenty of positive reviews, with all shopper comments so far being five stars. It does, however, mean that anyone hoping to read more mixed reviews will struggle.

One shopper said: "Really pleased with this wreath. Quality is great and looks very realistic. Sets my door off perfectly. Good for all year round use too, not just for Christmas." A second wrote: "This looks more expensive than it is. It's really full and natural looking."

View 3 Images All customer reviews so far are five stars (Image: Dunelm)

A third wrote: "This is the 2nd of these ivy wreaths I've bought, first one for the front door and then a second for the side gate, so I think that proves how 5* worthy I think they are! Lovely items and so worth the money. You won't be disappointed." Another said: "Beautiful wreath, looks like real ivy, definitely worth the money."

Article continues below

Someone else put: "Absolutely stunning! So happy with this wreath and have been waiting for a while for it to come back in stock. In my opinion nothing needs adding as it's already perfect. We'll worth the wait and worth every penny. 5*" One more said: "Did not expect much as price was low but I was pleasantly surprised, really like the wreath would order again. I added some white artificial flowers."

The wreath is 50cm in diameter, which may not be ideal for anyone with a big front door. Dunelm also advises that the wreath be wiped clean with a soft cloth, meaning shoppers will need to regularly check the wreath for maintenance. The store also adds an extra £3.95 on top for its standard delivery fee, meaning shoppers will have to pay a bit more. However, click-and-collect is free.

News.title: Martin Lewis 'fix now' warning as cheap energy deals vanish 'by the minute'
News.content:
Martin Lewis 'fix now' warning as cheap energy deals vanish 'by the minute'

A near 80% surge in wholesale energy costs has led to suppliers pulling cheap tariffs

View 3 Images A wave of cheap fixed rate energy deals have been removed in recent days (Image: Daisy-Daisy via Getty Images)

Cheap fixed rate energy deals are vanishing “by the minute” amid the fall-out from the Middle East conflict.

‌



Wholesale energy prices have surged by almost 80% since the US and Israel launched attacks on Iran last Friday, with Iran reacting through counter attacks of its own.

‌



Markets have been spooked by the threat of oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG) supplies through the Strait of Hormuz being halted.

‌



The UK is among countries that imports LNG to help meet the country’s gas needs, and is being impacted by the global rise in prices.

View 3 Images If you're on a standard energy tariff there are still cheaper fixed deals on offer (Image: Getty)

Energy suppliers have responded by pulling fixed rate energy tariffs that could have saved households who are on Ofgem’s price cap hundreds of pounds a year.

‌



Price comparison website Moneysupermarket says the number of such energy tariffs that have either been removed or repriced has now risen 57 in the past 72 hours.

Consumer champion Martin Lewis told viewers of his ITV show: “If you are on the price cap, the risk averse thing to do is to get a cheaper fixed as soon as possible.

“The cheap new fixes that are available over the last couple of days have either been pulled or repriced substantially upwards so if you’re going to do this soon, you need to do this now.

‌



“Last week all the cheapest fixes were between 10% and 14% less than the current cap. Today there is one at 9.5% and one at 6% and one at 3%. These are being pulled literally by the minute.”

The cheapest tariff available is E.ON Next’s Fixed 15 m v7 tariff, with an average annual cost of £1,533 - £225 less than the current price cap, and £108 cheaper than the new cap coming into effect in April. The deal is available exclusively to members of MoneySuperMarket’s SuperSaveClub.

View 3 Images Martin Lewis says cheap energy deals are "being pulled literally by the minute” (Image: ITV)

‌



At present the price cap is over £500 more than when it was first introduced in 2019, with MoneySuperMarket research finding nearly half (49%) of households had rationed their energy usage in the past 12 months.

The website is urging households to consider fixing now to lock in a below price cap deal and beat potential future prices rises this year.

Laura Hinton, from MoneySuperMarket Energy, said: “While the energy price cap is coming down from next month, this is largely due to readjustments on green levies, rather than wholesale energy costs themselves.

‌



“The market was initially predicting energy costs would remain relatively stable before slightly rising towards the end of the year. Instead, recent developments in the Middle East have pushed wholesale gas prices to a 12-month high, up 13% compared with 2025 levels.

“As a result of this uncertainty, we’re already seeing below-cap fixed deals being withdrawn. While the April price cap adjustment will safeguard customers from price hikes for now, energy price rises are now more likely later in the year.

“There are still some price-cap beating fixed deals on the market that can save as much as £108 a year against the current price cap.

Article continues below

“Though it’s possible that wholesale prices may stabilise over the next few weeks, the best way for people to protect themselves against rises later in the year and beat the price cap, is to switch and fix now.”

News.title: Teen boy 'raped' in Paisley city centre attack as cops launch frantic manhunt
News.content:
Teen boy 'raped' in Paisley city centre attack as cops launch frantic manhunt

Police have given a description of a man they are hunting following a "serious sexual assault" on a teenage boy in Meetinghouse Lane, Paisley, on Tuesday night

View Image The boy was attacked in Paisley (Image: Google)

A manhunt is underway over a sex attack on a teenage boy in a UK town centre.

Police were alerted to the "serious sexual assault" on Meetinghouse Lane, in Paisley, Glasgow, around 8.15pm on Tuesday, March 3.

The suspect is described as white, around 30 years old, with long brown hair. He was wearing a black gilet, light-coloured hooded top, grey jogging bottoms and black and grey Nike shoes.

Officers remain on the scene as enquiries continue. Investigators are particularly keen to speak with anyone who may have seen two males in the County Square area around 8pm.

Detective Inspector David Hardie said: “This was a terrifying incident for the victim, and it’s vital we trace the man responsible.

“I’m appealing to anyone with information to come forward. We’re particularly keen to speak to anyone who may have seen two males, one matching the suspect description, in the County Square area around 8pm.

“I’d also ask drivers with dash-cams or residents with private CCTV or doorbell cameras to check your footage and bring anything of significance to our attention.”

Anyone with information should contact Police Scotland on 101 quoting reference 3664 of 3 March. Alternatively, call Crimestoppers anonymously on 0800 555 111.

Article continues below

This is a Breaking News story. You’ll be more likely to see our stories when any big news breaks in future by simply by clicking this link . You can also join The Mirror’s WhatsApp Community or

News.title: Travel expert Simon Calder update for people with Dubai, Qatar or Abu Dhabi flights booked
News.content:
Travel expert Simon Calder update for people with Dubai, Qatar or Abu Dhabi flights booked

Simon Calder has issued crucial advice for thousands of Brits stranded following flight cancellations in the wake of the US-Israeli attacks on Iran

View 2 Images A plume of smoke rises after a strike on the Iranian capital Tehran as strikes continue

A travel expert has provided guidance for passengers with bookings on airlines operating to or from the Middle East. Thousands of British travellers remain stuck following the US-Israeli military action against Iran.

‌



Several airports shut down after Iranian counter-strikes, while major carriers, including British Airways (BA), Etihad, and Qatar Airways, scrapped services to and from the region. It is estimated that approximately 300,000 Britons are in the area, with roughly 100,000 in need of repatriation.

‌



Services have slowly restarted, with Emirates operating seven flights back from Dubai, while Virgin has one from Dubai to London Heathrow. Emirates has seven flights returning to the UK, with a further two from Abu Dhabi. BA is operating an evacuation flight to Heathrow from Oman.

‌



Travel specialist Simon Calder, appearing on GB News, explained that Britons are gradually returning home - but there is considerable progress still needed. He stated: "There are today (Wednesday) five Emirates flights scheduled to run from Dubai International Airport.

Content cannot be displayed without consent

"The vast majority of course of British people are in Dubai; they don't want to take a five-hour drive across the desert. And I very much hope that Emirates, which is putting out those five flights - there's two to Heathrow, one to Birmingham, one to Gatwick and one to Edinburgh - I very much hope that they are going to be prioritising people with a very urgent need to get home.

‌



"Virgin Atlantic have also got their first flight since the weekend going out later today to London Heathrow. So there are flights going; we've seen some from Etihad in Abu Dhabi as well. So it's a good sign. However, in terms of the sheer scale, it is barely scratching the surface.

"What you've got is the situation of an awful lot of people who are going to be finding that they are in a long, long queue. The Emirates has been putting under people's doors at hotels pieces of paper saying, 'look, these flights are going, let us know if you want to go on one'. It's a little bit kind of rudimentary, but people are leaving.

"There is a British Airways flight from Muscat which is one that they have organised commercially; there's also a Government flight. But I hesitate to use the term 'performative'. However, the main thing is, if you are talking about tens of thousands of people there, you need some real heavy lifting. And that's where the Emirates flights are coming in. But of course, their planes are scattered all over the place."

‌



Mr Calder revealed he had recently arrived back from Australia, where numerous Britons are spending 'thousands' on flights home via the US. He mentioned there were travellers stranded in locations including 'Japan to China to Cape Town in Africa' who are all attempting to return home. He stated 'lots more plans' were required, and it was 'desperate times'.

The UAE Government has apparently confirmed that there are 'corridors where they can guarantee the safety of 48 flights per hour'. Mr Calder said this was nowhere near sufficient to meet demand.

Regarding what passengers with tickets should do, Mr Calder advised: "In terms of refunds, do not take a refund unless you absolutely have already bought a good-value ticket. The airlines want you to cancel and get a refund because then you stop being their problem; the contract is annulled. So please don't do that.

‌



View 2 Images Strikes on Iran by the US and Israel are continuing (Image: Getty)

"Do not drive to Oman unless you are booked on one of the special flights which is being organised. Stay where you are; there's a Foreign Office warning saying shelter in place, so stay there. And look, help will be along soon. It's just a question of trying to boost the number of flights, but the fact that we have got five Emirates flights going to various UK airports as well as the Virgin Atlantic flight is significant.

"I just desperately hope, of course, that the pace can be accelerated and that people finally can get out in the next few days. But that will still leave a heck of a mess, particularly for all those people who are stranded across Asia, Africa and Australasia."

Article continues below

Approximately half a million passengers utilise airports in Dubai, Doha or Abu Dhabi daily, which serve as crucial hubs for travel between Europe and the continents of Asia and Australia. Specialists predict it could take weeks to clear the backlog of passengers.

Data from aviation analytics firm Cirium reveals that 603 out of 1,040 flights to Dubai were cancelled on Wednesday, implying a similar number of outbound flights from the city have also been scrapped.

News.title: 'I visited Ian Huntley's bulldozed home - a resident's reply took my breath away'
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: 'I visited Ian Huntley's bulldozed home - a resident's reply took my breath away'

As Ian Huntley fights for his life, the Mirror paid a visit to the village of Soham, where locals remain determined to forget the double child murderer and his obliterated House of Horrors

Soham killer Ian Huntley seriously injured in prison attack

Quiet and picturesque, the village of Soham conjures up images of fetes and cosy cafes. Nobody would guess it was the site of one of the most horrific crimes in British history.

‌



Now, Soham is once again in an unwelcome spotlight, following the brutal prison attack on Ian Huntley, whose foreboding shadow locals remain determined to keep at bay.

‌



On August 4, 2002, Holly Wells and Jessica Chapman walked away from a family barbecue to fetch sweets from a local leisure centre. With Jessica newly returned from a holiday in Menorca, the best friends were happily reunited once more and couldn't have looked more in sync as they strolled along together, clad in matching Manchester United shirts. "Two little Beckhams", noted one motorist, who became one of the last people to see the girls alive.

‌



The two ten-year-olds hadn't told anyone they were going for a walk when they set off from Holly's family home at approximately 6.15 pm. Walking through this leafy Cambridgeshire village, it's easy to see why they would have felt perfectly safe, meandering along together in the summer sunshine, weeks of carefree freedom stretching out ahead of them before school was set to start again.

View 4 Images Locals remain determined to keep Huntley's forboding shadow at bay. (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

As prosecutor Richard Latham QC would later put it: "No doubt they felt secure and confident in an area with which they were entirely familiar. [...] They lived there, they went to school there, they would have no doubt thought they were surrounded by faces they knew. Shortly after 6.30 pm, they simply vanished."

‌



Just like many children across the country, Holly and Jessica knew to be cautious of stranger danger. But the man responsible for their deaths was not a stranger, but the live-in boyfriend of their teaching assistant, Maxine Carr. A caretaker employed by the local secondary school, Ian Huntley, took advantage of his position in the most grotesque manner imaginable.

Huntley lured Holly and Jessica into his house, situated just next to the secondary school where he worked, under the promise that Carr was inside. In fact, she was 100 miles away in Grimsby, partying with another man. Photos would later emerge of the then 25-year-old kissing a teenage rugby player, on the day she would later claim she was at home with Huntley. It was here, in this ordinary property a short walk from the village centre, that Huntley murdered the much-loved children, likely by asphyxiation.

At around 20:30 pm that evening, Holly's parents, Nicola and Kevin Wells, went to check on the girls - who they believed were still playing upstairs - after guests remarked on how quiet they were being. They were nowhere to be found, and all too quickly, Nicola and Kevin were plunged into every parent's worst nightmare. The alarm was raised, and a search began, leading to more than 400 police officers descending on Soham. Sickeningly, Huntley and Carr put themselves at the heart of the narrative, painting themselves as simply a community-minded couple who wanted to bring the girls home.

‌



A missing persons' poster was displayed in their front window, while Carr even showed BBC Look East reporters a card Holly had made for her. The very picture of concern, Carr, who drew suspicion by referring to the youngsters in the past tense, recalled: "No one believes they would ever run away. They were very close to their families. This is something that I will keep for the rest of my life." Meanwhile, Huntley, then 28, left journalists feeling uncomfortable during the several odd interviews he gave during the search.

View 4 Images As the search for Holly and Jessica began, Huntley and Carr shamelessly placed themselves at the heart of the narrative (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

Brian Farmer, a PA news reporter who interviewed Huntley and Carr at their scrupulously cleaned home, was so alarmed by Huntley's sinister answers that he contacted the police. Huntley, who scarcely knew Holly and Jessica, "jumped in" to answer a question posed to Carr, with Farmer remembering: "He said that he thought Holly would probably get in the car and quietly go, but Jessica wouldn't. She'd put up a real fight and a real struggle." He added, "I think the way he described how Holly and Jessica would react is exactly how they did react. He knew how they'd react because that's how they reacted when he killed them."

‌



Following his subsequent arrest and conviction, the image of Huntley's home soon became a familiar sight on the evening news, with those watching outraged that a monster such as Huntley could have worked so closely with young people, despite having already faced serious allegations. It was a modern-day horror story that would change the country, with more stringent checks introduced for those entering professions involving children.

On the day of the Mirror's visit, no trace of the beast's humdrum lair remains. Like other houses of horrors before it, the property was bulldozed in 2004 to avoid it becoming a grim landmark for the morbidly curious. Even the crumbled bricks were removed to secret locations, to stop dark tourists from picking off macabre mementoes. There is no memorial to Holly and Jessica here - no one in the area wants the girls remembered in connection with the killer who cut their bright, joyful lives so brutally short.

View 4 Images Locals say 'people have tried to move past' the tragedy (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

‌



Standing before what was once 5 College Close, it's difficult to believe that a building once stood here. I almost worry that we've made some sort of dreadful mistake. There's not a piece of rubble to be seen, just a well-tended patch of lush green grass, partially concealed by trees. And this is exactly how residents would prefer it. Speaking anonymously with the Mirror about the attack on Huntley, one local took my breath away, commenting: "I think people have moved on. And I think people have tried to move past it, really, rather than still dwelling on everything that's happened. Just looking at the positives that have come out of it, things like DBSs in school, and more thorough checks and things like that. I don't think locally people are really talking about it that much."

It's early March when the Mirror takes a walk along College Close, and already, there is a sense of spring freshness in the air. The sun has begun to tentatively peek out, and you can visualise what it might feel like for a moment to be a child growing up in this pretty, slightly old-fashioned village, heading out for sweets with your best friend on an idyllic summer's day.

College Close is no dingy, desolate lane where pedestrians fear to tread. It's a pleasant, bustling road, teeming with dog walkers who stop and chat, while shouts of children's laughter ring from the school field. There is no sense of foreboding here, which appears to have lifted long ago, along with the heavy presence of Huntley. While the evil caretaker remains a national bogeyman, those who lived through the events seem understandably intent on wiping him from local memory altogether, and understandably aren't happy about more attention being shone on him in light of his grim injuries.

‌



View 4 Images Huntley is fighting for his life after being attacked behind bars (Image: Andy Stenning/Daily Mirror)

After all, as one person noted, Huntley really wasn't in the area very long, marking a terrible yet short moment in the historic village's long history. "Good luck" seems to be the general advice to reporters visiting determinedly tranquil Soham, where long-gone Huntley has no place among the waddling ducks, thatched cottages, and sweetly chiming church bells. No tears will be shed for the killer here, and locals remain resolute not to waste their anger, or so much as a shudder, on him either.

Do you have a story to share? Email me at julia.banim@reachplc.com

News.title: Family of Liverpool man fighting for life in Thailand give 'significant' update
News.content:
The family of a young Brit fighting for his life in Thailand after a horror scooter crash have issued a major update.

Zak Forsyth, 21, from Huyton, Merseyside, was left seriously injured last month when the scooter he was riding collided with a car. His girlfriend Amy Ekin, 21, said he was on his way back from an outing and sober while riding the scooter. Zak was taken to hospital by the driver of the car after the crash left him with a bleed on the brain, as well as a broken nose, jaw, ankle and arm.

He has been receiving treatment in Thailand ever since, with his family facing mounting medical bills as he did not have travel insurance. So far, Zak has undergone major facial reconstruction surgery as multiple bones in his face were shattered in the collision, as well as metal plates inserted and his jaw wired shut.

Image: Supplied) Supplied)

Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

He also had a tracheotomy and was breathing independently after initially being placed on a ventilator. Amy previously told how Zak was making "remarkable" progress, having taken medication that turned dangerous blood clots in his brains into fluid.

But in a tragic update shared via a JustGiving fundraiser launched by the family, Amy said Zak's condition has now taken a serious turn.

"Unfortunately, Zak's condition has not improved," she explained. "Last night he took a significant turn and began displaying very aggressive behaviour. His mental state is currently in a critical condition.

Image: LIVERPOOL ECHO) LIVERPOOL ECHO)

"While we initially hoped to admit him to a private hospital, the costs are simply beyond what we can afford. This is an incredibly difficult time for our family. Our priority is ensuring Zak's safety and getting him stabilised with the right medical support.

"We are taking this step because his health and wellbeing must come first. We are so grateful for the continued support, prayers and donations. Every contribution is helping us cover medical bills and ensure Zak receives the care he urgently requires."

Zak remains in intensive care following the crash, reports the Liverpool Echo. "We continue to need as much support as possible to help Zak regain strength before we can bring him home to Liverpool," Amy added.

"We would be extremely grateful if people could continue sharing the JustGiving page, as we do not know what tomorrow may bring.

"If Zak's condition worsens and surgery becomes necessary, the hospital will demand immediate payment before proceeding. This has been one of the most distressing experiences for our family, with staff standing in his ICU room with a card machine."

Anyone wishing to donate to the fundraiser can do so here.

Boxing.title: Inside the incredible plans to transform the Pyramids of Giza into a heavyweight boxing venue for Oleksandr Usyk's historic fight
Boxing.content:
Inside the incredible plans to transform the Pyramids of Giza into a heavyweight boxing venue for Oleksandr Usyk's historic fight

Oleksandr Usyk will swap packed stadiums for one of the most extraordinary settings sport has ever seen when he takes on former kickboxing champion Rico Verhoeven on May 23.

The two men are set to clash in front of the Pyramids of Giza with the WBC heavyweight world title on the line after Mauricio Sulaiman confirmed the green and gold belt will be at stake.

The WBC president has granted Usyk special permission to make a voluntary defence of his title before fulfilling his mandatory obligation, a bout against interim champion Agit Kabayel.

For Usyk, May's event will represent a slightly unconventional chapter in a career defined by taking on the biggest challenges in the most hostile environments.

Verhoeven, meanwhile, will dip his toe into boxing's ultimate proving ground and his first outing in a professional boxing ring for a decade after years of kickboxing dominance.

For organisers, it will be more than just a fight. It will be a global event, designed to merge elite combat sport with one of the most iconic backdrops on Earth.

Oleksandr Usyk will swap packed stadiums for one of the most extraordinary settings sport has ever seen when he takes on former kickboxing champion Rico Verhoeven on May 23

The two men are set to clash in front of the Pyramids of Giza (Verhoeven pictured above)

When Tiesto took to the decks on the Pyramids of Giza last December, the ancient plateau was transformed into something resembling a sci-fi movie set.

Lasers sliced through the sky, vast LED screens rose from the sand, and the pyramids themselves were illuminated like the backdrop to a Hollywood movie.

Yet for all the spectacle, what mattered most was what you couldn't see.

Behind the scenes, the entire operation ran like a military exercise. The stage was completely temporary - no digging, no drilling, no permanent fixings.

Every truss, lighting tower and screen was carefully weighted and balanced to ensure the desert surface (and the buried archaeology beneath it) remained untouched.

It's believed a fight involving Usyk would follow exactly the same blueprint: a raised, freestanding ring platform constructed entirely above ground and dismantled within hours of the final bell.

One obvious concern is noise. Heavyweight boxing is not exactly subtle.

But during Tiesto's show, engineers developed a 'heritage-safe' sound system that directed bass frequencies away from the pyramids.

When Tiesto took to the decks on the Pyramids of Giza last December, the ancient plateau was transformed into something resembling a sci-fi movie set

The WBC president has granted Usyk special permission to make a voluntary defence before fulfilling his mandatory obligation, a bout that must come against Agit Kabayel

For Usyk, May's event will represent a slightly unconventional chapter in a career defined by taking on the biggest challenges in the most hostile environments

Real-time vibration sensors monitored the limestone structures throughout the night to ensure sound levels stayed within strict archaeological limits.

A boxing fight would produce nothing like the sustained low-frequency rumble of a three-hour electronic set. Even so, vibration monitoring would almost certainly be in place again as an added safeguard.

The logistics would be just as tightly controlled. For Tiesto's event, guests entered via the Grand Egyptian Museum before being transported by dedicated shuttle to the secured concert zone on the plateau. A fight night would be expected to follow a similarly high-end access plan.

The concert footprint included a vast main arena space, VIP table sections, hospitality and brand activation zones, plus food and beverage areas spread across the sand.

For boxing, that layout could easily translate into a ringside enclosure carved into the desert, elevated hospitality decks and dramatic broadcast lighting tailored for a global pay-per-view audience.



Security, too, was uncompromising. Mandatory bag checks, controlled access points and a strict no re-entry policy were enforced throughout the concert - measures that would likely be even tighter for a world-title bout staged in the shadow of the last surviving wonder of the ancient world.

Boxing.title: Anthony Joshua takes a shot at Conor Benn in Instagram post as Brit shows his support for long-time promoter Eddie Hearn after former stablemate left Matchroom for rival Zuffa Boxing
Boxing.content:
Anthony Joshua has waded into the ongoing feud between Conor Benn and Eddie Hearn, lending his support to the promoter.

Benn, son of British legend Nigel, sensationally announced last month that he was ditching Hearn's promotional team Matchroom Boxing after a decade-long relationship - leaving for his rival Dana White's outfit Zuffa Boxing instead.

Hearn has since spoken out about that decision, revealing he was left 'devastated' by Benn having guided the British boxer from his 2016 debut to headlining cards and sticking by him after failing two drug tests in 2022.

And on Tuesday another long-term Matchroom Boxing fighter in Joshua showed his support to Hearn.

Uploading to his Instagram story, the former two-time heavyweight world champion shared a picture of him and Hearn dressed sharply.

Accompanied with that was a caption which read: 'AJ: Did you bring the fire power Eddie?

Anthony Joshua (left) took to Instagram on Tuesday to lend his support for Eddie Hearn (right) after Conor Benn left his Matchroom Boxing for rivals Zuffa Boxing last month

Benn (top) stunned Hearn when he decided to end their 10-year working relationship

'Eddie: It's tucked on my waste line mush [sic]

'AJ: I'll let you know when I have a clear sight of Connor Benn [sic]'.

When it was announced that he was splitting from Hearn, Benn - who has ambitions of winning the WBC welterweight title - thanked the 46-year-old for standing by him during the tough periods of his career and said he was excited for a 'new chapter' aimed at securing 'legacy fights' on the biggest stages.

However, speaking to iFL TV after Benn's bombshell announcement, Hearn claimed he was informed of the decision by the boxer's lawyer before being refused a phone call with him.

'Me personally, pretty devastated,' Hearn said of his reaction to the news. 'I have had a few days to digest what might happen. It is not often you get shocked.

'I blame myself. I forgot it was boxing. I just felt the loyalty that we have shown would never ever put us in this position.'

Daily Mail Sport reported that Hearn is thought to have lent Benn £500,000 following the collapse of his 2022 match-up with Eubank Jr, which came about due to his positive tests.

Hearn added that he 'misjudged the character' of Benn by feeling the 'loyalty' he had shown him 'would be enough to talk it through or get close to a number'.

He continued: 'It feels a little bit of a wasted few years. I gave a lot... I believed him and I backed him and I never gave in when he was done, out. Loaned him hundreds of thousands of pounds, but yeah on we go.'

'I just think the way it was done was very very wrong,' he added. 'But everyone is different. Some people, loyalty really matters, other people maybe not so.

'The way it played out to receive an email from the lawyer and then to ask for a call and not the willingness to have it, you just lose a bit of your soul to it.'

Benn's first bout as a Zuffa Boxing fighter will be against Regis Prograis on April 11 at the Tottenham Hotspur Stadium.

Boxing.title: Aussie boxer reveals that he received shocking death threats following the controversial ending to his fight with Nikita Tszyu
Boxing.content:
Aussie boxer Michael Zerafa has revealed he received multiple death threats after the controversial ending to his fight against Nikita Tszyu, adding that the online reaction ‘broke’ him.

The 33-year-old threatened to walk away from boxing following the no-contest, after he was booed out of the Brisbane Entertainment Centre in January, but has since revealed he is eager to return to challenge some of the biggest names in the sport.

Fans were left fuming after his fight against Tszyu in January ended prematurely when Zerafa had told a ringside doctor that he couldn’t see out of his left eye after accidentally clashing heads with his opponent.

Later, during a post-fight interview, Zerafa said that the ‘doctor had called it off’. The saga then took another controversial twist, with the Aussie boxer stating that his vision was just ‘blurry’, before adding that he wanted to continue fighting.

Spectators inside the Brisbane Entertainment Centre booed the Australian while some threw cans towards the ring.

Michael Zerafa (pictured) has revealed that he received 20 death threats in the 48 hours following his controversial fight with NikitaTszyu

The bout was called a no-contest after Zerafa told a ringside doctor that he couldn't see out of his left eye after clashing heads with Tszyu (left)

Tszyu has since categorically ruled out a rematch.

‘I'm closing the book on it. I'm just disappointed for everybody involved. I'm gutted for everyone,’ Tszyu said.

After the fight, Zerafa claimed in a social media post that he had suffered a shattered eye socket and damage to his retina. He had spent time in a Brisbane hospital, but his team did not disclose the doctor’s findings.

The 33-year-old has now spoken for the first time since the bout, but did not comment on the moment where he declared that he couldn’t see.

Speaking to The BillyJoe Podcast, he said: ‘Still to this day I don’t understand [the hate].

‘People say: “It’s because you b****ed it”, but I didn’t.

‘I had a genuine injury. I was winning the fight.’

After the bout, Zerafa issued an apology to fans, claiming that his choice of words was ‘poor’.

Zerafa had revealed that he had suffered a shattered eye-socket on social media following the fight

Zerafa (left) revealed that the criticism in the aftermath of the bout 'broke' him

He also doubled down on the legitimacy of his injury, stating that his vision was only 6/20. He added that ‘there was an issue with my eyesight, but it does not change the fact that my wording was poor.’

That apology has since been removed from his Instagram.

In the immediate aftermath of the bout, fans took to social media to send him abusive messages, with Zerafa revealing that some had even gone to his sponsors to ask why they were partnering with the Australian.

‘It broke me. I was on top. I’ve given my absolute all for this,’ Zerafa told the podcast. ‘When all that subsided, to see death threats, memes…

‘In that 48 hours, I reckon I copped 20 [death threats],’ he added.

‘We’re talking 48 hours. You get so much crap, man.

‘All directed at me. For what?’

He was pressed further on what kind of threats he received.

‘It was: “When I see you, I’ll make sure I’ll cut your throat, I know where you train, be expecting me there”.’

‘They reach out to sponsors and say: “As if you’re sponsoring this guy”.

‘Sometimes it makes me just want to slap these people. How much do you hate your life?

‘I get you might be jealous, but go and be jealous quietly.’

Questions have mounted over his future in the sport, with Zerafa’s relationship with No Limit Boxing said to have deteriorated.

But the 33-year-old, who holds a 34-5-0 record, added that he is rebuilding and is determined to challenge for a world title.

While he has kept a very low profile over the past two months, the fighter took to Instagram over the weekend to publish a video announcing that he was working hard to get back to competing.

He even shot a dig at those who had criticised him, writing in the caption of the video: 'And yeah... I can see now.'

Zerafa has now announced that he is hoping to get back to the ring and challenge the best in the game, poking a dig at his critics by writing on Instagram: 'And yeah... I can see now'

‘I’m not done yet. I know a lot of people would like to see me done, but I’ve still got a fire burning,’ Zerafa said during the podcast, when asked about what the future holds.

‘I’ve got a lot of people to prove wrong, I’m hungrier and better things are coming.

‘I’ve cleaned out my backyard, it’s time to get back to where I belong, back with the bigger guys, back where people know what they’re doing.

‘Boxing is a beautiful sport, but there’s too many people with their hands in it who are destroying it.

‘And I don’t even need to say names.’

Boxing.title: Revealed: Anthony Mundine's startling courtroom act as he has a bizarre clash with judge
Boxing.content:
Revealed: Anthony Mundine's startling courtroom act as he has a bizarre clash with judge

Faced a Sydney court over a driving charge

Anthony Mundine has left a Sydney courtroom stunned after performing an acknowledgement of country and refusing to follow standard plea procedure during a tense exchange with a magistrate.

The former rugby league star and world champion boxer appeared at Sutherland Local Court last Tuesday, where he represented himself on a charge of driving during a disqualification period.

Before proceedings began, Mundine performed an acknowledgement of country and addressed the court in unusual terms.

'I'm Anthony of the tribe Mundine, here in my private capacity,' he said.

He also acknowledged the 'Dharug people of the Eora Nation'.

The court was then drawn into a procedural standoff when Mundine declined to enter a formal plea to the charge.

Anthony Mundine caused a scene when he appeared in a Sydney court over a charge of driving during a disqualification period

The former NRL and boxing star conducted an acknowledgement of country and refused to enter a plea to the charge

Magistrate Jillian Kiely explained that the legal process required a clear plea of either guilty or not guilty.

'If you fail to make a plea, I can't make a plea of not guilty,' the magistrate said.

Mundine refused to comply with the court's direction.

'I don't consent … I'm innocent,' he said.

Because Mundine would not formally enter a plea, the magistrate entered a not guilty plea on his behalf in accordance with court procedure.

The 50-year-old also attempted to have the charge dismissed, but the magistrate informed him that she did not have the authority to withdraw the matter. Only police could make that decision.

'They [police] will be held accountable … I'm joking,' he said.

The court appearance was the latest in a string of motoring offences Mundine has racked up

The court appearance follows a string of motoring offences stemming from a police stop near Mundine's home.

Authorities allege he was driving while disqualified and operating a vehicle without proper registration and insurance.

Mundine has indicated he intends to challenge the matter on broader legal grounds.

'To elaborate, the matter pertains to the attempted legal restriction of fundamental rights,' he said.

He cited 'the right to travel, the right to peace, the right to freedom of association, and the right to equality before the law, including the right to be heard and seen equally in the eyes of the law'.



After leaving the courtroom, Mundine briefly shadow-boxed for waiting media but declined to provide further explanation about his defence strategy.

Boxing.title: Inside my wild night at a Las Vegas casino with Floyd Mayweather: Boxing legend splashed out on caviar, fine whiskey and dropped $500 tips galore - but he baulked at spending his cash on post-night snack, writes Daily Mail's CHARLOTTE DALY
Boxing.content:
Former world champion Floyd Mayweather Jr has announced a surprise return to the ring, revealing plans for a fourth comeback that will begin with an exhibition against Mike Tyson before a blockbuster professional rematch with Manny Pacquiao later this year.

The rivals are set to meet again on September 19 at the Sphere - 11 years after their long-awaited 2015 showdown, when Mayweather claimed a unanimous-decision victory in the so-called 'Fight of the Century.'

Pacquiao, now 47, returned to action in July 2025 and fought Mario Barrios to a majority draw for the WBC welterweight title, insisting his comeback is driven by a desire to become world champion again. Mayweather, meanwhile, says he 'still has what it takes to set more records,' despite questions regarding his financial status.

A recent report by Business Insider suggested Mayweather’s widely publicised fortune may be overstated, citing liens, outstanding debts, foreclosures and heavily leveraged assets including property holdings and his private jet.

Back in 2024, I caught a glimpse of life inside Mayweather's orbit - a whirlwind casino evening filled with luxury excess, eye-watering tips and one surprising moment of frugality.

Floyd Mayweather invited Daily Mail Sport for a night out in Las Vegas in an extraordinary offer

Charlotte Daly hit the strip with Mayweather for a big night out in Las Vegas (pictured above)

When Floyd Mayweather invites you for a night out in Las Vegas, you know it will be extraordinary. Picture this: the neon lights of the strip reflecting off luxury cars, the pulsating energy of the city that never sleeps, and 'Money Mayweather' at the centre of it all.

From exclusive cigar lounges to high-roller tables, this was no ordinary night out. We sipped 'Good Money Whiskey' and indulged in caviar before discussing Mayweather's relationship with his father, the key moments in his career, the current crop of boxers, and his exhibition earnings.

In true Mayweather style, he arrived at Eight Lounge, a chic cigar spot, in a blacked-out SUV, wearing a £2,000 Gucci jacket and a gleaming Rolex. Fans clamoured for photos as he was greeted by models holding sparklers and a glowing Mayweather sign.

'I do like the finer things in life', Mayweather quipped when asked about his love for luxury. 'I enjoy my cars and my watches but money don't make me, I make money.

'I don't just keep all the money either, I give back to the less fortunate. My mother don't want for nothing, my loved ones don't want for anything. My team live a pretty good life. They travel the world, stay at five star hotels. But, the most important thing is, we are happy. I was happy before I made money. With or without money I am a happy person.'

A fixture on the Vegas Strip with his 7,000-square-foot gentleman's club and top-tier boxing gym, Mayweather took charge behind the bar, crafting an Old Fashioned cocktail with his Good Money Whiskey - a brand now available in 10 states including Nevada, California, and Florida.

When handed the five-year-old whiskey, Mayweather joked, 'She's come all the way from the UK, and you're giving her the five-year-old whiskey? She deserves the 10-year!'

Though not drinking himself, Mayweather passed the cocktail with a knowing smile. 'It's just like boxing,' he said confidently. 'I had the best team, so I got the best results. I've got the best team working on my brands, too. Just try it and you'll see.'

In true Mayweather style, he arrived at Eight Lounge, a chic cigar spot, in a blacked-out SUV, wearing a £2,000 Gucci jacket and a gleaming Rolex

With a drink in hand, we moved to Mayweather's exclusive area, adorned with Le Bon Argent Champagne and attentive waitresses ensuring my glass was always full. Intrigued by highlight reels of his career playing on the screens overhead, I asked the boxing legend about his favorite memory.

'I'm not sure which one was my favourite fight but the best fighter I ever fought probably was Manny Pacquiao. I can see why he won so many fights, it's all the moves he would make. But, I reckon the roughest fight I ever had was Emanuel Augustus, he was very tough competitor. When I faced him, I think he was 26 with 14 losses but extremely tough.

'That being said, I think around half of my 50 fights were world title fights, so they were all good fights'.

Mayweather faced and defeated some of the biggest names in boxing, including Manny Pacquiao, Oscar De La Hoya, and Canelo Alvarez. He also claimed world titles in five different weight classes: Super Featherweight, Lightweight, Light Welterweight, Welterweight, and Light Middleweight.

But the true highlight of his career was walking away unharmed—a poignant topic given his father and uncle, Roger, both suffered neurological damage resulting in memory loss.

'The real highlight of my career is that I am still able to communicate. That I am still able to speak well. I am still able to read and write. I am able to spend time with my children and I am able to travel. I still have all my faculties. That's the part about my career I am most pleased with. Not everyone gets that luxury and I've seen it first hand.'

Mayweather's relationship with his father has been complex but pivotal. Mayweather Sr. was instrumental in shaping his son's career, developing the defensive style that became his hallmark. Despite public disagreements and periods of estrangement, their bond remains strong.

'My dad was my inspiration growing up. Without my dad I wouldn't have any of this. Without my dad I wouldn't have the crazy real estate portfolio I have. I wouldn't have met my two best friends, James McNair and Ricki Brazil. I wouldn't be here with you today. My dad is my driving force, my dad is everything to me.'

Fans clamoured for photos with him as he made his way to Eight Lounge, a chic cigar spot

A stark contrast to when Mayweather lashed out at his father in 2011, shouting: 'You couldn't fight for s***. You weren't nothing but a motherf***ing cab driver... bum a** trainer. You weren't s*** as a fighter, so how you gonna be something as a trainer. Get the f*** out my gym f*****t!'.

While other kids played, Mayweather's childhood was a whirlwind of responsibilities, juggling time between caring for his drug-addict mother and training with his father.

'My dad was about business, ever since I was a kid. From 1985, my dad had me running five miles a day. He wouldn't let me run in sneakers either. He made me run five miles every day in combat boots while I was a child.

'He also brought me out into the garden and made me chop wood. He always told me, there is no room for excuses. He said no matter how many fights you win, it's never good enough, you can always get better. There is always room for growth.

'But, my dad gave me structure. He helped mould me into the man I am today. A man of honour, a man of dignity. A stand-up guy. A person that believes he can get better. It's all because of my father.'

As we chatted, one of Floyd's friends casually offered spoonfuls of caviar around the room. This wasn't your average caviar; it was the kind that likely costs more than my entire monthly salary. The nonchalant manner in which it was served, however, made it seem like an everyday snack.

Meanwhile, Eddie Griffin, a comedian from American sitcom Malcolm & Eddie, flitted around the room casually cracking jokes before diving into the high-stakes action at the casino tables.

Having accompanied him to the high-roller area, I watched in awe as players casually dropped $500k per round on craps. It was clear I'd be spectating rather than participating in this high-rolling spectacle.

A fixture on the Vegas Strip with his 7,000-square-foot gentleman's club and top-tier boxing gym, Mayweather took charge behind the bar, crafting an Old Fashioned cocktail
